title: Epigastric pain radiating to the back in a poorly-controlled diabetic

chart.oneLiner: 50M with poorly-controlled type 2 diabetes, hypertriglyceridemia, and hypertension presents with 8 hours of severe constant epigastric pain radiating to the back, with nausea and vomiting, on a background of a recent alcohol binge and several missed doses of his triglyceride-lowering medication.

opening: I've got this terrible pain in the pit of my stomach that bores straight through to my back.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 50-year-old man, in significant pain, sitting forward/curled up, breathing a bit fast. Cooperative but distracted by pain.

HPI: 8h of severe, constant epigastric pain boring through to the back, WORSE lying flat, somewhat better leaning forward. Persistent nausea and several episodes of vomiting. The pain came on a few hours after a weekend of heavy drinking. NO hematemesis, NO black/tarry stools, NO chest pressure or pain radiating to the arm/jaw, NO tearing/ripping back pain, NO sudden collapse or feeling faint. Bowels last opened yesterday, passing gas. He feels his heart racing and is breathing hard because of the pain.
PMH: type 2 diabetes for ~10 years, poorly controlled (last A1c he was told was 'high, around 10'); high triglycerides/cholesterol; high blood pressure; one prior admission a few years ago for 'pancreas inflammation' after a binge. NO known gallstones, NO prior abdominal surgery. (Reveal these only if asked about medical history.)
MEDS (reveal only if asked what he takes, and which ones he actually takes): metformin (often skips it); fenofibrate for triglycerides — RAN OUT about three weeks ago and has not refilled it; hydrochlorothiazide and lisinopril for blood pressure; an over-the-counter 'fat-burner / weight-loss' supplement a friend gave him. NO regular NSAIDs, NO steroids, NO new antibiotics. ALLERGIES: none.
SOCIAL: drinks ~6–8 beers most days, escalating over the past month, and binged heavily this weekend; smokes half a pack a day; eats a lot of fast food, admits poor diet. Works as a long-haul driver. (Reveal alcohol/tobacco/diet only if asked about them.)
FAMILY: father had diabetes; no family pancreatic or aortic disease. ROS: nausea/vomiting as above; no fevers/chills volunteered; no urinary symptoms; no leg swelling; no recent diarrhea; subjectively passing less urine today and feeling very thirsty.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: distressed, diaphoretic, tachypneic, dry-looking.
- Vitals: BP 108/70, HR 116, RR 24, 37.9°C, SpO2 95% RA. (If orthostatics checked: HR rises >20 and BP drops on standing — volume down.)
- Mucous membranes/skin: dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor (volume depletion).
- Abdomen: marked epigastric tenderness with voluntary guarding; reduced bowel sounds; mild distension; NO rebound or rigidity elsewhere; no palpable pulsatile mass. No flank (Grey Turner) or periumbilical (Cullen) ecchymosis.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Lungs: clear but reduced air entry/dullness at the left base if examined.
- Peripheral pulses: symmetric femoral and pedal pulses (no asymmetry to suggest dissection).

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the heavy alcohol use, the missed fenofibrate, the diabetes/triglyceride history, the thiazide, the weight-loss supplement, or the poor diet unless asked about alcohol, diet, medical history, or specific medications.
- Reveal exam findings only when that exam is performed; otherwise give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.Lipase: Lipase ~2.5x the upper limit of normal — elevated, but note it is NOT dramatically high; in hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis the assay can be falsely lowered by the lipemic sample, so a only-modestly-elevated lipase does NOT exclude severe pancreatitis. Diagnosis still met (≥3x not required when pain is characteristic and imaging supports).
labs.Triglycerides: Triglycerides markedly elevated at 4200 mg/dL (>1000 strongly implicates hypertriglyceridemia as the precipitant; serum grossly lipemic).
labs.CBC: WBC 16.5, Hgb 17.5 / Hct 53% (hemoconcentration from third-spacing), Plt 240. Hemoconcentration is an early severity marker.
labs.BMP: Na 138, K 4.2, Cl 100, HCO3 16, BUN 34, Cr 1.6 (baseline ~0.9 → acute kidney injury, prerenal). Glucose 410. Calculated anion gap = 138 − (100 + 16) = 22 (elevated anion-gap metabolic acidosis).
labs.Calcium: Total calcium 7.4 mg/dL (low; albumin normal so truly hypocalcemic) — from fat saponification; contributes to a prolonged QT on the EKG.
labs.VBG_lactate: VBG: pH 7.28, HCO3 16, anion-gap metabolic acidosis; lactate 3.6 mmol/L (hypoperfusion/third-spacing). A mild ketosis/early DKA picture is possible given glucose 410 — check beta-hydroxybutyrate.
labs.LFTs: AST 95, ALT 70, alkaline phosphatase normal, total bilirubin normal. ALT is NOT >150 and ALP/bilirubin are normal, arguing AGAINST a gallstone (biliary) cause.
labs.CT_abdomen: CT (deferred early; obtained only if diagnosis unclear or no improvement): diffuse pancreatic enlargement with peripancreatic fat stranding and a small amount of peripancreatic fluid; NO necrosis or organized collection on this early scan; aorta normal caliber with no aneurysm/dissection; no free air.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia with a prolonged QT interval (long QTc) from hypocalcemia; ST/T morphology otherwise non-ischemic. No inferior ST elevation to suggest an inferior MI mimicking the pain.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: sinus tachycardia with a markedly prolonged QT interval; no acute inferior ST-elevation. Self-host with appropriate credit.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing sinus tachycardia with a long QT interval and no acute ischemic ST elevation
ekg.clinicalVignette: A man with severe pancreatitis and a low serum calcium has a fast rhythm with a stretched-out QT interval on his EKG.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray (upright)
cxr.findings: Possible small left pleural effusion and basal atelectasis; no free air under the diaphragm.
cxr.verify: Pick a CC file showing a small basal/left pleural effusion or a normal upright film; must NOT show free air.
cxr.imageDescription: upright chest radiograph showing a small left pleural effusion with basal atelectasis
searchTerms: small left pleural effusion chest x-ray
cxr.clinicalVignette: A man with upper abdominal pain and fast breathing has an upright chest film.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis). Also explicitly name three distinct etiologies/mechanisms of acute pancreatitis you would want to sort out in THIS patient.
steps.rubric: Acute pancreatitis (leading,7). Perforated peptic ulcer (CANT-MISS,3). Inferior MI (CANT-MISS,3). Mesenteric ischemia (CANT-MISS,2). Ruptured/leaking AAA or aortic dissection (CANT-MISS,2). Acute cholecystitis / choledocholithiasis (core,1). DKA presenting as abdominal pain (core,2). Names ≥3 etiologies of pancreatitis relevant here — alcohol, hypertriglyceridemia (TG>1000), drug-induced (thiazide/HCTZ), gallstones (core,3). Named leading dx w/ justification — epigastric-to-back pain, binge + missed fibrate, prior episode (core,2). PENALTY: anchoring on alcohol alone and ignoring the triglyceride/drug precipitant (-2); calling it definitely gallstone without biliary labs (-1).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Acute pancreatitis. Must-not-miss alternatives to exclude with the EKG/CXR/labs: Perforated peptic ulcer, Inferior MI, Mesenteric ischemia, and a Ruptured/leaking AAA or aortic dissection (back pain). Also consider acute cholecystitis/choledocholithiasis and DKA presenting as abdominal pain (glucose 410). Three etiologies/mechanisms to sort out in HIM specifically: (1) alcohol (weekend binge, daily use); (2) hypertriglyceridemia — he ran out of fenofibrate ~3 weeks ago and has a TG >1000; (3) drug-induced — hydrochlorothiazide both raises triglycerides and is itself a recognized cause; gallstones are less likely given normal ALT/ALP/bilirubin. The competing fork is alcohol-induced vs hypertriglyceridemia-induced pancreatitis — both are defensible until the triglyceride level returns.

coreActions.item: Names ≥3 etiologies of pancreatitis — alcohol, hypertriglyceridemia, thiazide/drug, gallstones
Named leading dx w/ justification — epigastric-to-back pain, binge + missed fibrate, prior episode

penalties.item: Anchoring on alcohol alone and ignoring the triglyceride/drug precipitant
Calling it definitely gallstone without biliary labs

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Lipase (CANT-MISS,4). Triglyceride level — given the picture, essential to confirm the precipitant (CANT-MISS,3). BMP incl. calcium/glucose + VBG/lactate to catch AKI, anion-gap acidosis, hypocalcemia (core,3). CBC for hemoconcentration (core,1). LFTs to assess biliary cause (core,1). EKG (1). CXR (1). RUQ ultrasound for gallstones (bonus,1). Beta-hydroxybutyrate given glucose 410 (bonus,1). PENALTY: routine early CT in a clear-cut clinical/biochemical diagnosis (low yield) (-1); shotgun panel w/ no reasoning (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; Lipase; Triglycerides; CBC; BMP (with calcium and glucose); a VBG with lactate and a beta-hydroxybutyrate (glucose 410); LFTs; right-upper-quadrant ultrasound to look for gallstones. Defer CT abdomen/pelvis early — reserve it for diagnostic uncertainty or failure to improve. Be aware that a lipemic sample can falsely LOWER the measured lipase in hypertriglyceridemia, so a only-modestly-elevated lipase does not exclude the diagnosis.

penalties.item: routine early CT in a clear-cut clinical/biochemical diagnosis (low yield)

contraindicatedTests: routine early CT in a clear-cut clinical/biochemical diagnosis (low yield)

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and relate them to his labs.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia with a PROLONGED QT interval (long QTc) from hypocalcemia, and — importantly — NO acute inferior ST-elevation to suggest the inferior MI that was on the differential. CREDIT: identifies sinus tachycardia and the long QT (7); attributes the long QT to hypocalcemia (low calcium 7.4 from saponification) and flags torsades risk (5); explicitly states there is no acute ischemia/inferior STEMI, excluding the cardiac mimic (3). PENALTY: missing the prolonged QT / calling the tracing normal (-3); over-reading it as an acute MI (-2).
keyFindings: sinus tachycardia with a prolonged QT interval
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia with a prolonged QT (long QTc) from hypocalcemia; no acute inferior ST-elevation.
commonMistakes: over-reading the tracing as an acute inferior MI
not linking the long QT to the low calcium
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia with a prolonged QT interval (long QTc). The QT prolongation fits his hypocalcemia (calcium 7.4 from fat saponification in pancreatitis) and puts him at risk for torsades — correct the calcium and avoid further QT-prolonging drugs. Critically, there is NO acute inferior ST-elevation or other ischemic change, which argues against the inferior MI on the differential as the cause of his epigastric pain.

criticalActions: Sinus tachycardia with a prolonged QT (long QTc) from hypocalcemia; no acute inferior ST-elevation.
coreActions: sinus tachycardia with a prolonged QT interval

penalties.label: misses the prolonged QT / calls the tracing normal / over-reads as MI

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = small basal/left pleural effusion or atelectasis (a recognized finding in pancreatitis); NO free air under the diaphragm. CREDIT: identifies a small left effusion/basal atelectasis or a normal film (5); specifically excludes free air (perforated viscus) (5). PENALTY: missing the importance of excluding free air, or inventing pathology (-1).
keyFindings: identifies a small left effusion/atelectasis or a normal film
no free air under the diaphragm
criticalFindings: Possible small left pleural effusion and basal atelectasis; no free air under the diaphragm.
commonMistakes: missing the importance of excluding free air
inventing an infiltrate on a clear film
steps.idealAnswer: Possible small left pleural effusion and basal atelectasis — a recognized sympathetic finding in acute pancreatitis. Importantly, there is no free air under the diaphragm to suggest a perforated viscus.

criticalActions.item: Possible small left pleural effusion and basal atelectasis; no free air under the diaphragm.

coreActions.item: identifies a small effusion/atelectasis or a normal film and excludes free air

penalties.item: missing the importance of excluding free air

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the labs and EKG, what is your diagnosis, what is the most likely etiology, and how do you assess severity? Name the discriminating test and any residual ambiguity.
steps.rubric: Acute pancreatitis, most likely HYPERTRIGLYCERIDEMIA-induced (TG 4200, missed fenofibrate) with alcohol as a contributing precipitant (5). States the diagnosis is met by 2 of 3 (characteristic pain + lipase + imaging) and that the discriminating test was the triglyceride level — and notes the lipemic-sample pitfall that can falsely lower lipase (3). Assesses severity and the concurrent problems: AKI (BUN 34/Cr 1.6), hemoconcentration (Hct 53), anion-gap metabolic acidosis with lactate, hypocalcemia driving the long QT, hyperglycemia/possible early DKA, SIRS (2).
steps.idealAnswer: Acute pancreatitis — most likely hypertriglyceridemia-induced given a triglyceride of 4200 mg/dL after running out of fenofibrate, with the weekend alcohol binge and his thiazide as contributing precipitants. The diagnosis is met by 2 of 3 criteria (characteristic epigastric-to-back pain plus supportive lipase and CT findings); the discriminating test that resolved the alcohol-vs-triglyceride fork was the triglyceride level. Residual ambiguity: a lipemic sample can falsely lower the measured lipase, so the only-modestly-elevated lipase should NOT have been falsely reassuring; and gallstones, though unlikely with normal ALT/ALP/bilirubin, are formally excluded by RUQ ultrasound. This is NOT mild disease: he has multi-system involvement — AKI (BUN 34, Cr 1.6) and hemoconcentration (Hct 53) marking severity, an anion-gap metabolic acidosis (gap 22) with a lactate of 3.6, hypocalcemia (7.4) that explains the prolonged QT on his EKG, hyperglycemia (410) with possible early DKA, and SIRS physiology.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including how you would treat the underlying cause and your disposition/safe-discharge thinking?
steps.rubric: Per MGH pancreatitis: goal-directed IV fluid resuscitation — lactated Ringer's, titrated to BUN/HCT and urine output, avoiding over-resuscitation after 24–48h (CANT-MISS,6). Analgesia (3); antiemetics (2); early enteral nutrition as tolerated (2). Treat the underlying cause — lower triglycerides (insulin infusion +/- apheresis for TG>1000 with end-organ injury; restart fibrate once eating), manage hyperglycemia/possible DKA, hold the thiazide and the weight-loss supplement, counsel alcohol cessation (CANT-MISS,5). Correct hypocalcemia and monitor the QT / telemetry (2). Monitor for severity / organ failure, ICU if worsening (2). Disposition: ADMIT (not safe to discharge) — med reconciliation (stop HCTZ/supplement, restart fibrate, diabetes regimen), alcohol-cessation support, dietitian/lipid follow-up, close PCP follow-up (3). PENALTY: prophylactic antibiotics without infection (-3); discharging this patient home (-3); giving a QT-prolonging antiemetic without checking/correcting calcium (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Goal-directed IV fluid resuscitation with lactated Ringer's, titrated to BUN/HCT, lactate clearance, and urine output, with reassessment to avoid over-resuscitation after the first 24–48h. Analgesia and antiemetics (be cautious with QT-prolonging antiemetics given his long QT/low calcium — correct calcium and use telemetry). Early enteral nutrition as tolerated. TREAT THE CAUSE: lower the triglycerides — an insulin infusion (with glucose monitoring) and/or plasmapheresis for a triglyceride >1000 with end-organ injury, then restart fenofibrate once he is eating; manage the hyperglycemia/possible early DKA; HOLD the hydrochlorothiazide (worsens triglycerides) and stop the weight-loss supplement; counsel and support alcohol cessation. Correct the hypocalcemia and monitor the QT on telemetry. Monitor severity (SIRS, organ failure, AKI) and escalate to ICU if he worsens. Disposition: ADMIT — this is not safe to discharge given AKI, acidosis, hemoconcentration, and a long QT. Safe-discharge planning for later: medication reconciliation (stop HCTZ and the supplement, restart the fibrate, optimize the diabetes regimen), alcohol-cessation resources, dietitian/lipid-clinic referral, and close primary-care follow-up.

criticalActions.item: Goal-directed IV fluid resuscitation (lactated Ringer's, titrated to BUN/HCT/urine output)
Treat the cause — lower triglycerides (insulin infusion +/- apheresis), hold thiazide/supplement, alcohol cessation

coreActions.item: Correct hypocalcemia and monitor QT on telemetry
Monitor severity (SIRS/organ failure/AKI); ICU if worsening
Admit with safe-discharge planning: med reconciliation, alcohol-cessation support, dietitian/lipid + close PCP follow-up

bonusActions.item: Manage hyperglycemia / treat possible early DKA

requiredActions: Goal-directed IV fluid resuscitation (lactated Ringer's, titrated to BUN/HCT/urine output)
Treat the cause — lower triglycerides (insulin infusion +/- apheresis), hold thiazide/supplement, alcohol cessation
optionalActions: Correct hypocalcemia and monitor QT on telemetry
Monitor severity (SIRS/organ failure/AKI); ICU if worsening
Admit with safe-discharge planning: med reconciliation, alcohol-cessation support, dietitian/lipid + close PCP follow-up
Manage hyperglycemia / treat possible early DKA
steps.disposition: Admit; ICU if severe (AKI, acidosis, organ failure)

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 50M with poorly-controlled type 2 diabetes, hypertriglyceridemia, and hypertension presents with 8 hours of severe constant epigastric pain radiating to the back, with nausea and vomiting, after a weekend alcohol binge and ~3 weeks off his fenofibrate. The diagnosis is Acute pancreatitis — most likely hypertriglyceridemia-induced (TG 4200) with alcohol and a thiazide as contributing precipitants — reached by eliciting the focused history (alcohol, missed fibrate, thiazide, diet), performing the targeted exam, interpreting a directed workup (lipase, triglycerides, BMP/calcium/VBG), and recognizing the concurrent multi-system problems (AKI, anion-gap acidosis, hypocalcemia with a long QT, hyperglycemia) while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives (perforated ulcer, inferior MI, mesenteric ischemia, AAA/dissection).
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: marked epigastric tenderness with voluntary guarding; reduced bowel sounds; mild distension; no rebound or rigidity elsewhere; no palpable pulsatile mass. No flank/periumbilical ecchymosis.
Abdomen: marked epigastric tenderness with voluntary guarding; reduced bowel sounds; mild distension; no rebound or rigidity elsewhere; no palpable pulsatile mass. No flank/periumbilical ecchymosis.
Abdomen: marked epigastric tenderness with voluntary guarding; reduced bowel sounds; mild distension; no rebound or rigidity elsewhere; no palpable pulsatile mass. No flank/periumbilical ecchymosis.
Dry mucous membranes and poor skin turgor, consistent with volume depletion from third-spacing and vomiting.
Orthostatic: HR rises >20 bpm and BP drops on standing — volume depletion.
Abdomen: marked epigastric tenderness with voluntary guarding; reduced bowel sounds; mild distension; no rebound or rigidity elsewhere; no palpable pulsatile mass. No flank/periumbilical ecchymosis.
Symmetric femoral and pedal pulses; no asymmetry or pulse deficit to suggest aortic dissection.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals (tachycardia, tachypnea, narrowing BP).
Elicit the focused history, including alcohol, the missed fenofibrate, the thiazide, and diet only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal epigastric peritonism, volume depletion, and symmetric pulses.
Build a differential led by Acute pancreatitis while explicitly excluding perforated ulcer, inferior MI, mesenteric ischemia, and AAA/dissection, and name three etiologies of pancreatitis.
Order a focused workup (lipase, triglycerides, BMP/calcium/VBG) and interpret the long-QT EKG, the CXR, and the multi-system labs.

Commit to hypertriglyceridemia-induced acute pancreatitis, assess severity, and manage with goal-directed fluids, cause-directed therapy (lower triglycerides, hold thiazide, alcohol cessation), QT/calcium correction, admission, and safe-discharge planning.
commonPitfalls: Anchoring on alcohol alone and missing the triglyceride/drug precipitant
Being falsely reassured by an only-modestly-elevated lipase in a lipemic sample
Discharging this patient home despite AKI, acidosis, and a long QT
Giving a QT-prolonging antiemetic without correcting calcium
managementPearls: Goal-directed IV fluid resuscitation (lactated Ringer's, titrated to BUN/HCT/urine output)
Treat the cause — lower triglycerides (insulin infusion +/- apheresis), hold thiazide, alcohol cessation
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia with a prolonged QT (long QTc) from hypocalcemia; no acute inferior ST-elevation.
Possible small left pleural effusion and basal atelectasis; no free air under the diaphragm.
criticalManagementItems: Goal-directed IV fluid resuscitation (lactated Ringer's, titrated to BUN/HCT/urine output)
Treat the cause — lower triglycerides (insulin infusion +/- apheresis), hold thiazide/supplement, alcohol cessation

Possible small left pleural effusion and basal atelectasis; no free air under the diaphragm.
imageInterpretationPearls: A prolonged QT in a pancreatitis patient should prompt a look at the serum calcium — saponification causes hypocalcemia.
On an abdominal-pain EKG, the deliberate pertinent negative is the absence of an acute inferior STEMI mimicking the pain.
On the chest film, exclude free air under the diaphragm and note a sympathetic basal effusion.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's pain and concern
Asks permission before sensitive questions (alcohol, adherence)
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
